question: What does scanner.hasNextInt() do?
options: A. Reads the next integer from input
B. Checks whether the next input token can be read as an integer
C. Converts the next token into an integer automatically
D. Clears invalid input from the scanner
explanation: hasNextInt() only checks the next token without consuming it. To actually read it, nextInt() must be called.
hasNextInt() returns false for "abc", so the else block runs and prints Invalid input.

options: A. It prints only valid integers and skips invalid tokens
B. It prints all input as strings
C. It causes an infinite loop
D. It converts all input to integers
explanation: If the next token is an integer it reads and prints it. Otherwise scanner.next() consumes and discards the invalid token.

question: What would happen if the else scanner.next() line were removed from the previous pattern?
options: A. The program would skip invalid input automatically
B. The program could get stuck on the same invalid token indefinitely
D. The invalid token would become an integer
explanation: hasNextInt() does not consume input. Without next() to discard it, the same invalid token remains and the loop runs forever.

question: Which statement about an abstract class is correct?
options: C. It cannot be instantiated directly
D. It must contain only abstract methods
explanation: Abstract classes can have fields, constructors, and regular methods, but you cannot create an object with new AbstractClass().

question:
Given: public abstract class Person {}

Which line is legal?
explanation: You may declare a reference variable of an abstract type, but you cannot instantiate it directly with new.
A superclass reference can point to a subclass object. This is upcasting and is always safe.

question: Why might an abstract class contain a regular method with a full implementation?
options: A. So subclasses can reuse or override it
B. Because abstract classes cannot contain abstract methods
C. Because Java requires every abstract class to have at least one regular method
D. So the abstract class can be instantiated
explanation: Regular methods in an abstract class provide shared behavior that subclasses can inherit directly or override.

options: A. Abstract classes cannot have constructors
explanation: A constructor must always have an implementation body. It cannot be declared abstract.

options: A. Abstract methods cannot be static
B. Static methods must always be abstract
C. Abstract methods must always be private
D. Static methods must return a value
explanation: Abstract methods require overriding through polymorphism, but static methods are not overridden polymorphically.

question: Which statement about abstract methods is correct?
options: A. They must have a body
C. They must be declared inside an abstract class
D. They can appear inside any regular class
explanation: An abstract method has no body and must live inside an abstract class. A regular class cannot contain abstract methods.

options: A. Legal, because Dog inherits sound()
B. Compilation error, because Dog must implement sound() or be declared abstract
C. Runtime error only when creating Dog
D. Legal only if sound() is private
explanation: A concrete subclass must implement all inherited abstract methods. Since Dog does neither, compilation fails.

question: How can the previous code be fixed without implementing sound() in Dog?
explanation: Declaring Dog as abstract postpones the requirement to implement sound() to a concrete subclass further down.
Bulldog is concrete and correctly implements the abstract method inherited through Dog, so Bark is printed.

options: A. Abstract methods cannot be private
B. Private methods must be abstract
C. Private methods cannot return void
D. Abstract classes cannot contain private members
explanation: A private method cannot be overridden by subclasses, but an abstract method must be implemented by subclasses — these two requirements contradict each other.

options: B. Animal then Dog then Bulldog then WOOF
C. Animal then Dog then Bark
explanation: Constructors run from superclass to subclass: Animal, Dog, Bulldog. Then sound() uses the runtime type Bulldog, so WOOF is printed.

question:
What is the compile-time type in this line?

Animal a = new Dog();
explanation: The compile-time type is always the declared reference type on the left — in this case Animal.

question:
What is the runtime type in this line?

Animal a = new Dog();
explanation: The runtime type is determined by what new actually creates — in this case a Dog object.

options: A. Legal, because the runtime object is B
B. Compilation error, because reference type A has no fun()
explanation: The compiler checks available methods using the compile-time type. Since A does not declare fun(), the call is rejected.

question: How can the previous code be fixed to call fun()?
explanation: Casting a to B tells the compiler to treat it as a B reference, giving access to fun(). The cast is safe since the actual object is B.

question: What is upcasting?
options: A. Casting from a superclass reference to a subclass reference
B. Treating a subclass object as a superclass reference
C. Calling a private method from a subclass
explanation: Upcasting moves from a more specific type to a more general type, such as assigning a Dog to an Animal reference. It is always safe and implicit.

question: Which line is a valid example of upcasting?
explanation: Animal a = new Dog() is valid upcasting — a Dog is an Animal, so the assignment is safe without any explicit cast.

question: What is downcasting?
options: A. Treating a superclass reference as a subclass reference
B. Treating a subclass as its superclass
C. Creating a static inner class
explanation: Downcasting converts a general parent-type reference to a more specific child-type reference, and requires an explicit cast.
The compiler allows the cast, but the actual object is an Animal, not a Dog, so a ClassCastException is thrown at runtime.
The actual object is a Dog, so casting the Animal reference back to Dog is valid and safe.

options: D. num stores the length of the string
explanation: The actual object is a String, not an Integer. The cast compiles but fails at runtime with a ClassCastException.

question: Which tool helps avoid unsafe downcasting?
explanation: instanceof checks whether an object is actually compatible with a target type before attempting the cast.

options: A. Legal, because the runtime type is Dog
explanation: The compiler checks the reference type Animal. Since Animal does not declare isBig(), the call is rejected at compile time.

options: A. Legal if the actual object is Dog
D. Legal only if Animal is abstract
explanation: Casting d to Dog tells the compiler to look for isBig() in Dog. Since the actual object is Dog the cast and call are safe.
The compiler allows the cast, but the actual object is an Animal, not a Dog, causing a ClassCastException at runtime.

question: What is method overriding?
options: A. Two methods with the same name but different parameters in the same class
B. A subclass provides a new implementation for a method inherited from its superclass
C. A method is declared as static
D. A method is hidden by a local variable
explanation: Overriding means a child class redefines an inherited method with the exact same signature to change its behavior.

question: What is method overloading?
options: A. Same method signature in subclass and superclass
B. Same method name with different parameter lists in the same class
D. Casting an object to another type
explanation: Overloading defines multiple methods with the same name but different numbers or types of parameters.
getTheFamily() is overridden in WildPlants. Dynamic dispatch selects the runtime type WildPlants, so WildPlants is printed.

options: A. Yes, because the parameter list is different
B. No, because the return type is the same
C. No, because method names cannot repeat
explanation: Overloading is valid when the parameter lists differ. Return type and access modifier are irrelevant to overloading.

options: A. The subclass reduces the access level from public to private
B. The subclass uses the same return type
C. Private methods cannot exist in subclasses
D. void methods cannot be overridden
explanation: When overriding, a subclass cannot reduce the visibility of the method. Access can be widened but never narrowed.

question: What is an interface mainly used for?
options: A. Defining required method signatures that implementing classes must provide
B. Creating objects directly with new
explanation: An interface defines a contract — a set of method signatures that any implementing class must fulfill.

question: What is the implicit modifier of methods declared in a Java interface?
explanation: Interface methods are implicitly public and abstract unless they use newer Java features such as default or static methods.

question: What is the implicit modifier of fields declared in a Java interface?
explanation: Interface fields are implicitly public static final, making them shared constants that cannot be changed.

options: A. TAX can be changed inside implementing classes
B. TAX is a public static final constant
D. Payment p = new Payment() is legal
explanation: Interface fields are public static final by default, so TAX is a constant that cannot be reassigned.
The reference type is Add, and Add does not declare sub(). The compiler rejects the call.

question: How can the previous code safely call sub()?
options: B. ((Sub)x).sub(2, 1) after checking x instanceof Sub
explanation: The object implements Sub, but the reference must be cast to Sub first. Checking instanceof Sub beforehand makes the cast safe.
The cast to Sub compiles because both are interfaces, but Cal does not actually implement Sub, so a ClassCastException is thrown at runtime.

question: Can a Java class extend more than one class?
options: B. No, Java supports only single class inheritance
C. Only if both classes are abstract
D. Only if one of them is an interface
explanation: Java deliberately does not support multiple class inheritance. A class can extend only one other class.

question: Can a Java class implement multiple interfaces?
options: D. Only if the class is abstract
explanation: A Java class can implement any number of interfaces, which is how Java achieves a form of multiple inheritance.

options: A. Illegal, because a class cannot extend and implement at the same time
B. Legal — one draw() implementation satisfies both the abstract class and the interface
C. Illegal, because interface methods cannot match abstract class methods
D. Legal only if Sketcher is abstract
explanation: Sketcher extends one class and implements one interface. The single public draw() method satisfies both requirements simultaneously.

question: What is a static nested class in Java?
options: A. A class inside another class that requires an outer object to be created
B. A class defined inside another class that is accessed through the outer class name
C. A method defined inside another method
D. An anonymous class with no name
explanation: A static nested class belongs to the outer class itself, not to any instance, and is created using OuterClass.NestedClass syntax.

options: C. A.B b = new A().new B();
explanation: Static nested classes are accessed through the outer class name. new A.B() creates an instance without needing an outer object.

options: B. A.B b = new A().new B();
explanation: A non-static inner class requires an existing outer object. new A().new B() creates the outer object first, then the inner object.

question: What can a regular non-static inner class access from its outer class?
options: A. Only static fields of the outer class
B. Only public fields of the outer class
C. Both static and non-static fields of the outer object, including private ones
D. No outer class fields at all
explanation: A regular inner class holds an implicit reference to its outer object and can access all its members, including private ones.
Generics require reference types, not primitives. int, double, and char are primitives — their wrapper classes Integer, Double, and Character must be used instead.
